ai_slop_vocabulary.insight: The market has developed a rich vocabulary around the problem, with 'AI slop' emerging as the dominant category label. 'Generic' and 'robotic' are the most common descriptors.

ai_tell_words.insight: Prospects can identify specific AI tell-words. 'Delve', 'moreover', 'furthermore', 'tapestry', and 'realm' are most frequently cited as red flags.

solution_vocabulary.insight: The dominant solution vocabulary centers on 'prompts' (720 mentions) and 'humanize/humanizer' (193 combined). 'Workflow' and 'system' suggest appetite for structured approaches.

journey_stages.description: Prospects start excited about AI writing tools, quickly become disappointed by output quality
journey_stages.representative_quote: I was so excited to use AI for my copy but after weeks of trying, everything still sounds robotic and generic

journey_stages.description: Prospects cycle through multiple AI tools hoping each new one will solve the quality problem
journey_stages.representative_quote: I've tried ChatGPT, Claude, Jasper, Copy.ai, and they all produce basically the same generic output

journey_stages.description: End up spending more time editing AI output than it would take to write from scratch
journey_stages.representative_quote: Fixing AI output takes longer than writing from scratch. The whole point was to save time.

journey_stages.description: Question whether to keep using AI or abandon it entirely, caught between efficiency and quality
journey_stages.representative_quote: I'm at the point where I'm questioning whether AI copy is even worth the effort

common_timelines.initial_trial: 1-2 weeks of excitement with a new AI tool
common_timelines.disillusionment: 2-4 weeks when output quality patterns become obvious
common_timelines.resignation_or_system: 3-6 months before either giving up or finding a systematic approach

primary_triggers: AI output all sounds the same
Paid tools don't deliver on promises
Brand reputation damage from AI slop
Being replaced by AI but unable to use it well
Competitors gaining advantage with better AI use
Finding a system that actually works
Copy that converts without manual rewriting
AI that captures personality and voice
AI companies pushing 'AI-powered' as marketing gimmick

common_actions.description: Endlessly tweaking prompts hoping for better output
Moving from one AI tool to another
Spending significant time manually fixing AI output
Running output through AI detectors before publishing
Trying to train AI on personal/brand voice

typical_mistakes: Treating AI as a vending machine (put in prompt, get perfect output)
Using the same approach across all content types
Relying solely on system prompts without reference material
Expecting AI humanizers to fix fundamental quality issues
Not providing enough context about target audience

limiting_beliefs.reality: Prompts alone cannot overcome model defaults. Reference material and calibration are needed.

limiting_beliefs.belief: AI humanizers fix the quality problem
limiting_beliefs.reality: Humanizers address surface patterns but don't fix structural or voice issues.

limiting_beliefs.belief: The tool is the problem (just need to find the right one)
limiting_beliefs.evidence: Multiple tool comparisons and switching behavior
limiting_beliefs.reality: All LLMs share similar default patterns. The approach matters more than the model.

limiting_beliefs.belief: AI copy will eventually be indistinguishable from human
limiting_beliefs.evidence: Recurring debates about AI vs human detection
limiting_beliefs.reality: Without systematic calibration, AI defaults to statistical averages.

limiting_beliefs.belief: You either use AI or write manually (false binary)
limiting_beliefs.evidence: Polarized discussion between pro-AI and anti-AI camps
limiting_beliefs.reality: The optimal approach is human-AI collaboration with proper system design.

shared_worldviews: AI is powerful but the output quality isn't there yet
Everyone's content sounds the same because they're all using AI the same way
Speed and quality are in tension (AI gives speed, humans give quality)
The market is flooded with AI-generated mediocrity
People who claim AI writes perfectly are either lying or have low standards
